cards.title: Behaviour Change Models — Clinical Application
cards._legacyHtml:
Core Principle
Prescribing lifestyle changes without assessing readiness is like prescribing antibiotics without a diagnosis. Match your intervention to the patient's stage of change.
StagePatient SaysClinician ActionGoal
Precontemplation"I don't have a problem"Raise awareness — personalised health risk information, non-judgementalCreate doubt
Contemplation"I know I should, but..."Explore ambivalence — decisional balance exercise (pros/cons)Tip the balance
Preparation"I'm going to change soon"Help plan — SMART goals, identify barriers, arrange supportFirm commitment
Action"I've started changing"Active support — positive reinforcement, problem-solve barriersSustain change
Maintenance"I've maintained for 6+ months"Relapse prevention — identify high-risk situations, coping strategiesPrevent relapse
Relapse"I went back to old habits"Normalise — "relapse is part of change" — identify triggers — restartRe-engage

cards.title: Motivational Interviewing — Core Techniques
cards._legacyHtml:
O
Open Questions "What concerns you most about your diet?" not "Do you eat well?" Open questions elicit elaboration, allow patient to set agenda, reveal motivation and barriers. Never start with advice — start with curiosity.
A
Affirmations Genuine, specific affirmations build self-efficacy. "You've managed to walk every day this week despite being busy — that takes real commitment." Not generic praise. Reinforce patient's strengths and past successes.
R
Reflections Reflect content: "So you find it hard to cook healthy when you're tired." Reflect feeling: "It sounds like you're frustrated that you keep starting and stopping." Double-sided: "On one hand you want to lose weight, on the other you enjoy eating out with family."
S
Summaries Collect and reflect back key themes: "Let me make sure I've understood — you know sugar affects your diabetes, you've tried cutting back before, and the main obstacle is stress at work leading to cravings. Is that right?" Ends with open question — invites correction and next step.
🟡 Change Talk vs Sustain Talk
Change talk: patient expresses Desire ("I want to"), Ability ("I could"), Reason ("It would help"), Need ("I have to"), Commitment ("I will"). Sustain talk: reasons to stay the same. Your job is to elicit and reinforce change talk, not argue with sustain talk. Arguing causes reactance — the patient digs in. Roll with resistance: "It sounds like now might not be the right time — what would need to be different for you to consider this?"

cards.title: SMART Goals & Compliance Strategies
cards._legacyHtml:
SMARTPoor ExampleGood Example
Specific"Eat better""Eat 3 servings of vegetables daily"
Measurable"Exercise more""Walk 30 min, 5 days per week"
Achievable"Run a marathon in 2 weeks""Walk 10 min every lunch break"
Relevant"Do 100 pushups" (for HTN patient)"Reduce salt to <5g/day" (for HTN)
Time-bound"Lose weight""Lose 2kg by next appointment (4 weeks)"
1
Habit Stacking Attach new health behaviour to existing habit. "After I brush my teeth (existing) → I do 5 min pranayama (new)." Removes need for willpower — relies on existing neural pathway as trigger.
2
Implementation Intention "When X happens, I will do Y." "When I feel stressed at work, I will do box breathing for 2 minutes." 2–3x more effective than goals alone (Gollwitzer 1999).
3
Remove Friction Make the healthy choice the easy choice. Pack gym clothes the night before. Keep cut vegetables in the fridge. Place water bottle on desk. Remove junk food from home. Environment design beats willpower every time.
4
Progress Tracking Self-monitoring doubles success rates. Simple paper diary or phone app. Focus on behaviour (days walked) not just outcome (weight) — outcome is delayed, behaviour is immediate. Review at every visit — celebrate small wins.
🟡 The 2-Minute Rule
For patients struggling to start: prescribe the 2-minute version of the habit. "Just put on your walking shoes and step outside for 2 minutes." The act of starting is the hardest part — once started, most patients continue. Gradually scale up over weeks. Never increase a habit by more than 10% per week to avoid overwhelming the patient.

cards.title: Health Coaching & Therapeutic Communication
cards._legacyHtml:
The Therapeutic Relationship IS the Treatment
40% of treatment outcome is determined by therapeutic relationship quality, independent of the actual treatment. How you communicate matters as much as what you prescribe.
1
The ICE Framework Before explaining your diagnosis, always explore: I — Ideas ("What do you think is causing this?"), C — Concerns ("What worries you most?"), E — Expectations ("What were you hoping we could do today?"). ICE takes 2 minutes and dramatically improves patient satisfaction and compliance. Patients who feel their ideas and concerns were heard are 3x more likely to follow recommendations.
2
Explaining Naturopathy to Sceptical Patients Avoid: "detox", "toxins", "energy", "vibrations" (triggers scepticism). Use: "evidence-based lifestyle medicine", "functional approach", "identifying root causes". Frame with: "Research shows diet and lifestyle changes have measurable effects on blood markers, inflammation, and symptoms." Lead with science, not philosophy.
3
Managing Non-Compliance Never shame or lecture. Ask: "What got in the way?" Explore practical barriers (time, cost), motivational barriers (ambivalence), knowledge barriers (misunderstood instructions). Modify the plan to fit the patient's life. A 50% compliant plan the patient follows beats a perfect plan they ignore.
4
When to Refer — Non-Negotiable Refer immediately: suicidal ideation, psychosis, acute medical emergency, unexplained weight loss, blood PR, dysphagia, new neurological symptoms. Refer routinely: severe depression, T1DM, active autoimmune flare, stage 2 uncontrolled HTN, diagnostic uncertainty, no improvement after 3 months. NEVER discourage patients from conventional medicine — collaborative care is best practice.
